query: urinary biomarkers such as NGAL and KIM-1 used to detect tubular lesions in solitary kidney

retrieved_docs.title: Diabetic Nephropathy -- Evaluation -- Urinary Biomarkers
retrieved_docs.content: Neutrophil gelatinase-associated lipocalin (NGAL) and kidney injury molecule-1 (KIM-1) are elevated in early diabetic nephropathy, even before the onset of albuminuria, and correlate with a decline in decreased GFR. [23] [38] Urinary KIM-1 is associated with proximal tubule damage, while NGAL is associated with damage to the loop of Henle and distal tubule. NGAL is also an early marker of acute kidney injury (AKI), with serum elevations detectable within hours of the causative insult and up to 24 to 72 hours before creatinine levels. [40] Urinary NGAL also appears before albuminuria. [23] [38] [39]

retrieved_docs.title: Perioperative Acute Kidney Injury -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Other biomarkers being studied for similar purposes are (NGAL), kidney injury molecule-1 (KIM-1), and cystatin C. NGAL has been especially well studied and is highly correlated with renal tubular injury. [30] NGAL prevents bacteria from absorbing iron, which inhibits their growth and multiplication. In AKI, NGAL levels are elevated in both plasma and urine up to 24 hours before elevated plasma creatinine levels. [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Diabetic Nephropathy -- Evaluation -- Urinary Biomarkers
retrieved_docs.content: The most studied biomarkers for diabetic nephropathy include NGAL, KIM-1, and periostin. One study found that NGAL had a sensitivity of 76% and specificity of 55%, KIM-1 had a sensitivity of 63% and specificity of 90%, and periostin had a sensitivity of 80% and specificity of 66%. [39] [41] Although many of these biomarkers are not yet widely available outside research settings, their use is becoming more established, and combining them may enhance early detection of diabetic nephropathy. Some of the potential biomarkers for diabetic nephropathy that have been studied over the last decade are listed in the Table below. Table reference [42]

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Renal Tubular Necrosis -- Evaluation -- Novel biomarkers
retrieved_docs.content: Numerous biomarkers have evolved to detect AKI/acute tubular necrosis earlier than serum creatinine. These biomarkers include serum cystatin C as an early and reliable marker of renal injury compared to a rise in serum creatinine, which is often witnessed 48 to 72 hours after the initial insult. [10] [18] Other markers include urinary α-one microglobulin, β-2 microglobulin, hemoxygenase-1, osteopontin, and kidney injury molecule 1. [10] [16] Urinary biomarker neutrophil gelatinase-associated lipocalin is reliably upregulated in renal ischemia after tubular injury and is one of the best-studied markers for kidney injury; it is considered sensitive and specific and begins to rise 2–6 hours after initial kidney injury, significantly earlier than creatinine. [37] [38] [39]

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Kidney Injury -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: There are markers of tubular function that can be calculated to help distinguish between prerenal, intrarenal, and postrenal causes, such as the fractional excretion of sodium and urea and urine osmolality; however, these parameters are affected by many drugs commonly used in clinical practice such as diuretics and are not always diagnostic. No single marker can be reliably used in isolation to distinguish among the causes of AKI, and clinical presentation must be considered.

retrieved_docs.title: Nephrotoxic Medications -- Monitoring -- Kidney injury Molecule 1
retrieved_docs.content: KIM1 is a type 1 glycoprotein rapidly detected in urine upon damage to tissue that allows for the measurement of proximal renal tubule damage. Greater sensitivity than traditional methods of measuring renal damage such as BUN, Creatinine, and the presence of proteinuria. [5] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Contrast-Induced Nephropathy -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Assessment of acute kidney injury with serum creatinine has poor sensitivity and specificity. Several new markers have been identified with most of the current interest focused on cystatin C, neutrophil gelatinase-associated lipocalin, interleukin 18, and kidney injury molecule 1.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Renal Tubular Necrosis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The workup usually differentiates acute tubular necrosis from prerenal AKI and other causes of AKI, such as acute interstitial nephritis. Common tests that help differentiate include urinalysis, response to fluid repletion, urinary sodium concentration, fractional excretion of sodium, and fractional excretion of urea in patients who receive diuretics and novel biomarkers.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Kidney Injury -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Although creatinine is the most commonly used serum chemistry to evaluate for AKI, there is evidence that other biomarkers may be more sensitive and may be elevated earlier in the course of AKI compared to creatinine. Some of these include neutrophil gelatinase–associated lipocalin (NGAL), which can be measured in the plasma and urine, kidney injury binding protein-1 (KIM-1), retinol-binding protein, cystatin C, α/β microglobulin, and urine uromodulin. The use of these biomarkers is not widely available and has not yet been validated in wide-scale studies. [14] [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Kidney Disease -- Evaluation -- Assessment of Glomerular Filtration Rate
retrieved_docs.content: Many other compounds are also being examined for use as markers of acute kidney disease and CKD, including cystatin C, kidney injury molecule 1 (KIM-1), soluble urokinase-type plasminogen activator receptor, urinary epithelial growth factor, beta-2 microglobulin, retinol-binding protein, serum neutrophil gelatinase-associated lipocalin (NGAL), L-type fatty acid-binding protein (L-FABP), fibroblast growth factor 23 (FGF23), and beta-trace protein. [24] In addition, the Chronic Renal Insufficiency Cohort (CRIC) study demonstrated that markers such as certain cardiac markers (high-sensitivity troponin T and NTproBNP), the plasma chemokine CXCL12, and urine NGAL were highly associated with the progression of CKD. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Kallmann Syndrome -- Evaluation -- Laboratory Findings in Kallmann Syndrome
retrieved_docs.content: Ultrasound can identify solitary kidneys.

retrieved_docs.title: Diabetic Nephropathy -- Evaluation -- Urinary Biomarkers
retrieved_docs.content: Given the relative nonspecificity and delayed utility of creatinine change and albuminuria as markers of diabetic nephropathy, other molecules are being explored as potential markers. In recent years, there has been growing interest in studying markers of tubulointerstitial injury rather than focusing solely on the glomerulus. Additionally, non-albuminuric proteinuria, which indicates tubulointerstitial injury, is strongly associated with DKD. Some evidence even suggests that proximal tubular damage may occur earlier than glomerular damage. [23] [38] [39]

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Function Tests -- Procedures -- Novel Biomarkers
retrieved_docs.content: Several new biomarkers have been reported to be useful for determining acute kidney injury and have utility in differentiation between acute kidney injury and stable CKD and pre-renal and intrinsic acute kidney injury. These biomarkers include low-molecular-weight proteins and fall into 2 main categories as follows: Proteins and chemicals present in the systemic circulation, which undergo glomerular filtration Cystatin C Beta-2-microglobulin Retinol-binding protein Recently recognized filtration markers pseudouridine, acetylthreonine, myoinositol, phenylacetylglutamine, and tryptophan [14] Proteins that are produced in response to cellular or tissue injury Neutrophil gelatinase–associated lipocalin (NGAL) Kidney injury molecule 1 (KIM-1) L-type fatty acid–binding protein (L-FABP) Fibroblast growth factor 23 (FGF23) Beta-trace protein The optimum clinical utility of these substances is further clarified through ongoing research.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Kidney Injury (Nursing) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: There are markers of tubular function that can be calculated to help distinguish an etiology like the fractional excretion of sodium and urea, urine osmolality, but the sensitivity of all these markers is very poor, and they are affected by many drugs very commonly used in clinical practice such as diuretics. Therefore, no single marker can be reliably used in isolation to distinguish pre-renal from renal causes of AKI, which is a common misconception in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Biopsy -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Kidney biopsy should not be performed if, based on a comprehensive noninvasive radiological workup, it has a low to zero chance of changing treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Kidney Injury -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: A kidney biopsy is an excellent but infrequently utilized tool. It is usually indicated in patients with rapidly declining renal function without apparent cause or when multiple etiologies of AKI are possible. It is a test with some associated risks, such as bleeding, and should be used cautiously if a solitary kidney or coagulopathy is present.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2842
retrieved_docs.content: Benign arteriolar nephrosclerosis and essential hypertension are clinically similar conditions, as both involve pathological changes in small arterial vessels that lead to elevated blood pressure and related end‑organ effects. Recognizing the overlap in their clinical presentations helps clinicians differentiate primary hypertension from secondary renal vascular disease, guides appropriate diagnostic evaluation, and informs management strategies aimed at controlling blood pressure and preserving kidney function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1995
retrieved_docs.content: Dehydration (التجفاف) is clinically associated with the development of acute kidney injury (فشل كلوي حاد). In turn, acute kidney injury is clinically linked to low blood volume (قلة حجم الدم), reflecting the importance of adequate intravascular volume in preserving renal function. Acute kidney injury also encompasses the condition of extracellular blood nitrogen (نيتروجينية الدم خارج الكلوية), indicating that disturbances in nitrogenous waste handling outside the kidneys are a relevant component of its clinical spectrum. Understanding these interconnections—how dehydration can precipitate acute kidney injury, how hypovolemia contributes to its progression, and how extracellular nitrogen abnormalities manifest—helps clinicians identify risk factors, monitor patients, and implement timely interventions to protect renal function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 2686
retrieved_docs.content: Acute kidney injury, acidosis, hyperkalemia, and extrarenal blood nitrogen are closely interrelated. Acute kidney injury is clinically associated with both acidosis and hyperkalemia, indicating that patients with sudden loss of renal function frequently develop metabolic acidosis and elevated serum potassium levels. In addition, acidosis and hyperkalemia commonly co‑occur, reflecting shared pathophysiological mechanisms in renal failure. Acute kidney injury also includes the condition termed extrarenal blood nitrogen, a manifestation of nitrogenous waste accumulation outside the kidneys. Recognizing these linked abnormalities is essential for timely diagnosis and comprehensive management of patients with acute kidney injury.

retrieved_docs.content: and may be performed with or without KUB or tomography. Percutaneous insertion of a nephrostomy tube targets the renal pelvis, providing minimally invasive drainage for obstructed or damaged kidneys. Collectively, these relationships delineate a structured network linking nephrotoxic substances, a broad spectrum of invasive and endoscopic interventions, hierarchical procedural classifications, and diagnostic imaging modalities such as intravenous pyelography, thereby supporting comprehensive patient care in renal and ureteral pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 921
retrieved_docs.content: Essential hypertension is identified and interpreted through increased blood pressure measurements, which are central to its clinical assessment. Benign arteriolar nephrosclerosis exhibits clinical features that are similar to those of essential hypertension, highlighting overlapping manifestations and the need to consider both conditions when evaluating patients with elevated blood pressure and related renal changes.

The same terminology groups acute kidney inflammation (التهاب الكلية الحاد, التهاب كبيبات الكلى الحاد) with acute renal failure (مجموعة الفشل الكلوي الحاد) and nests nephritis, nephrosis, and nephrotic syndrome under acute glomerulonephritis. Procedures directed at the urinary system are organized under “Procedure on urinary system.” Ultrasonography of the urinary and male genital systems (ULTRASOUND PROCEDURES ON THE URINARY AND MALE GENITAL SYSTEMS) includes kidney ultrasound (فحص الكلى بالموجات فوق الصوتية), bladder scan, and kidney‑ureter scan, providing essential diagnostic imaging for renal and urinary pathologies. Imaging of the biliary tree, such as intra‑operative cholangiogram, falls under biliary imaging (صورة الأوعية الصفراوية), paralleling the hierarchical structuring of procedural categories across organ systems. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2554
retrieved_docs.content: Benign arteriolar nephrosclerosis is classified as a subtype of اضطراب الأوعية الكلوية (renal vascular disorder) and shares clinical characteristics with essential hypertension, indicating that patients with this form of arteriolar kidney disease often present with blood‑pressure patterns and target‑organ effects similar to those seen in essential hypertension. Recognizing this relationship helps clinicians differentiate primary hypertensive disease from secondary renal vascular pathology and guides appropriate diagnostic evaluation and management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2469
retrieved_docs.content: Genitourinary symptoms and ill-defined conditions are classified as a subcategory of اضطراب السبيل البولي (urinary tract disorder). These nonspecific genitourinary manifestations frequently co‑occur with حصاة حالبية (kidney stone), indicating that patients presenting with such symptoms should be evaluated for possible calculi within the urinary system. Recognizing that genitourinary symptoms and ill-defined conditions belong to the broader umbrella of urinary tract disorders and are often associated with kidney stones helps clinicians prioritize diagnostic testing and management strategies aimed at identifying and treating underlying stone disease.

Overall, the knowledge base illustrates how abdominal and peritoneal surgical interventions, renal endoscopic and lithotripsy procedures, associated diseases, and a spectrum of diagnostic and therapeutic imaging techniques are interrelated, supporting comprehensive patient assessment and multidisciplinary care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2863
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant arteriolar nephrosclerosis is clinically similar to hypertension with complications and secondary hypertension, indicating that the severe renal vascular changes seen in malignant arteriolar nephrosclerosis share comparable clinical manifestations, risk factors, and disease mechanisms with complicated primary hypertension and hypertension that arises secondary to other underlying conditions. This similarity underscores the importance of recognizing overlapping signs and symptoms when diagnosing and managing patients with advanced hypertensive organ damage and secondary causes of elevated blood pressure.

Together, these interconnections illustrate how hemodialysis, its acute and chronic complications, related comorbidities, specific glomerular pathologies, systemic autoimmune or drug‑induced syndromes, and targeted ultrasonographic evaluation of the urinary system are integral to understanding renal pathology and guiding comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1596
retrieved_docs.content: Acute and unspecified renal failure, which is classified as فشل كلوي حاد, is clinically associated with الحماض (acidosis) and فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم (hyperkalemia). The presence of الحماض and فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم frequently co‑occurs, reflecting their linked pathophysiology in renal impairment. Moreover, acute and unspecified renal failure is considered related to, and possibly synonymous with, وكلاء الكليون السفلي (lower urinary tract agents), indicating an overlap in terminology or clinical context between these entities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 80
retrieved_docs.content: Nephrotoxic acute renal failure is recognized as a substance‑related cause of acute kidney injury, illustrating that various substances can precipitate renal pathology. The generic entity “Substance” participates in a wide array of invasive interventions, including muscle, tendon, bursa, joint and soft‑tissue injections and aspirations, as well as non‑therapeutic urinary‑tract procedures, male genital procedures, and therapeutic endocrine procedures. Within the urinary‑tract procedural hierarchy, ureteral catheterization functions as a central category from which specific techniques such as cystoscopic catheterization of ureter, ureteroscopic dilatation of ureter, URETER: INJECTIONS AND IMPLANTATIONS, and bilateral or unilateral endoscopic ureteric catheterization with fluoroscopic imaging are derived; these endoscopic catheterizations are also linked to other X‑ray examinations of the urinary system. “Cystoscopy and other transurethral procedures” serves as a parent node for these

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 163
retrieved_docs.content: illustrate the integration of hemodialysis, its complications, CKD subtypes, glomerular diseases, vascular anomalies, surgical interventions, and adjunctive therapies within clinical coding frameworks to support comprehensive patient assessment, management, and documentation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 4 -- Community 2863
retrieved_docs.content: Acute kidney failure (فشل كلوي حاد) is clinically associated with dehydration (التجفاف) and with abnormal mental states (حالة نفسية غير طبيعية). When renal function declines sharply, the condition can give rise to extrarenal blood nitrogen accumulation (نيتروجينية الدم خارج الكلوية), reflecting the buildup of nitrogenous waste products that the kidneys are no longer able to clear. These interrelated clinical features highlight the importance of recognizing fluid deficits and neuropsychiatric changes as potential indicators of acute kidney injury and of monitoring nitrogen waste levels to guide timely management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2341
retrieved_docs.content: Failure (biologic function) is associated with الفَشَلٌ الكَبِدِيُّ الكُلْوِيّ, indicating that a general loss of biological function can be linked to hepatorenal failure—a severe condition in which both liver and kidney functions are compromised. Recognizing this association is important for clinicians, as it highlights the need to assess and manage systemic organ failure comprehensively in affected patients.
